title: Westward expansion: economic development
description: Test your knowledge on the economic causes and effects of settlement of the West.

assessment_items.question_content:
Year | Acreage harvested | Production (in millions of bushels) | Price per ton
- | - | - | -
1867 | 16,738 | 211 | $\$2.01$
1869 | 21,194 | 288 | $\$0.92$
1871 | 22,230 | 272 | $\$1.25$
1873 | 24,866 | 322 | $\$1.17$
1875 | 28,382 | 314 | $\$1.01$

**A development related to the overall trend from 1866 to 1875 depicted in the table was the:**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: migration of farmers to the Great Plains region.
creation of free trade agreements with Asian countries.
introduction of a sharecropping system in the South.
discovery of precious metals and minerals in the West.

assessment_items.question_content:

- | - | - | -

**The overall trend from 1867 to 1875 depicted in the table was most directly caused by which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: improvements in farm mechanization
legislation that limited Asian immigration
elimination of protective tariffs for manufactured goods
innovations in steamboats to transport crops

assessment_items.question_content:
Year | Acreage harvested | Production (in millions of bushels) | Price per ton
- | - | - | -
1867 | 16,738 | 211 | $\$2.01$
1869 | 21,194 | 288 | $\$0.92$
1871 | 22,230 | 272 | $\$1.25$
1873 | 24,866 | 322 | $\$1.17$
1875 | 28,382 | 314 | $\$1.01$

*The table most strongly supports which of the following arguments?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: As agricultural production increased, the price of food declined across the United States.
As the price of food increased, agricultural production increased for farmers in the Great Plains.
As agricultural production increased, farmers cultivated less acreage as land became scarce.
As the price of food decreased, agricultural production decreased as family farms could not shoulder the costs.

assessment_items.question_content:
> “The object is to accomplish a thorough and systematic organization among farmers, horticulturists, and those engaged in rural pursuits throughout the United States, that will secure among them intimate social relations and acquaintance with each other, for the advancement and elevation of their pursuits, with an appreciation of their true interests. By such means may be accomplished that which exists throughout the country in all other vocations and among all other classes — combined co-operative association for the individual improvement and common benefit.”

>-Source: W. Scott Morgan, *History of the Wheel and Alliance and the Impending Revolution,* 1889

**The excerpt best serves as evidence of which of the following trends during the 1870s and 1880s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the creation of organizations aimed at connecting farmers at local and regional levels
the migration of families to western lands following the introduction of the Homestead Act
the construction of the transcontinental railroad that connected the East to the West
the introduction of federal policy to support the agricultural industry after a financial panic

assessment_items.question_content:
> “The object is to accomplish a thorough and systematic organization among farmers, horticulturists, and those engaged in rural pursuits throughout the United States, that will secure among them intimate social relations and acquaintance with each other, for the advancement and elevation of their pursuits, with an appreciation of their true interests. By such means may be accomplished that which exists throughout the country in all other vocations and among all other classes — combined co-operative association for the individual improvement and common benefit.”

>-Source: W. Scott Morgan, *History of the Wheel and Alliance and the Impending Revolution,* 1889

**In the late 1860s, the Grange focused its energies on which of the following strategies?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: increasing railroad regulations to cut transport costs for farmers
reducing crop production to increase the average cost of crops
securing government warehouses for farmers to store their crops
building canals and railroads to the West to transport all crops

assessment_items.question_content:
> “The object is to accomplish a thorough and systematic organization among farmers, horticulturists, and those engaged in rural pursuits throughout the United States, that will secure among them intimate social relations and acquaintance with each other, for the advancement and elevation of their pursuits, with an appreciation of their true interests. By such means may be accomplished that which exists throughout the country in all other vocations and among all other classes — combined co-operative association for the individual improvement and common benefit.”

>-Source: W. Scott Morgan, *History of the Wheel and Alliance and the Impending Revolution,* 1889

**The excerpt is best understood as a response to which of the following historical developments?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the rise in sales of crops to national and international agricultural markets
the increase in conflicts with indigenous communities that lived in the West
the migration of a religious group to present-day Utah and Nevada
the introduction of legislation that gave western land to families willing to cultivate it

assessment_items.question_content:
> “The present year witnesses the completion of the most important enterprise of the kind ever executed in any country— a line of railroad from the Missouri River across the Continent, and with connecting lines from the Atlantic to Pacific Ocean. . . . the influence of these works . . . upon the commerce and welfare of this country must be immense. A vast commerce, yet in its infancy, already exists between the two shores of the Continent.”

>-Source: Henry Varnum Poor, *Manual of the Railroads of the United States, 1869-1870,* 1889

**The author’s reference to “a line of railroad from the Missouri River across the Continent” most directly reflects which of the following policy changes?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: federal efforts to support the construction of the Transcontinental Railroad
political commitment to enact Henry Clay’s American System in the Southwest
federal efforts to rebuild southern infrastructure after the Civil War
legislation that reclaimed private land in the West for governmental use

assessment_items.question_content:
> “The present year witnesses the completion of the most important enterprise of the kind ever executed in any country— a line of railroad from the Missouri River across the Continent, and with connecting lines from the Atlantic to Pacific Ocean. . . . the influence of these works . . . upon the commerce and welfare of this country must be immense. A vast commerce, yet in its infancy, already exists between the two shores of the Continent.”

>-Source: Henry Varnum Poor, *Manual of the Railroads of the United States, 1869-1870,* 1889

**Which of the following most directly contributed to the developments described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: government loans to support railroad construction in the West
trade agreements and partnerships with European and Asian countries
federal legislation that gave land to families willing to work western land
United States victory over the Confederate states in the Civil War

assessment_items.question_content:
> “The present year witnesses the completion of the most important enterprise of the kind ever executed in any country— a line of railroad from the Missouri River across the Continent, and with connecting lines from the Atlantic to Pacific Ocean. . . . the influence of these works . . . upon the commerce and welfare of this country must be immense. A vast commerce, yet in its infancy, already exists between the two shores of the Continent.”

>-Source: Henry Varnum Poor, *Manual of the Railroads of the United States, 1869-1870,* 1889

**The events discussed in the excerpt led to which of the following economic changes?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the opening of new markets in North America
the increase in immigration from Asian countries
the introduction of high protective tariffs
the discovery of precious metals and minerals

assessment_items.question_content:
> “It is little more than four years ago since I was last in this then almost unknown, but now world-famous, oil region. In the comparatively brief interval that has elapsed everything but the geographic conformation of the country, everything but its mountains and rivers, has been changed. . . . 

> “Take one instance. Corry, four years ago, was a poor farm where the thinly-scratched soil of cold clay land yielded so little that the whole place, buildings and all, might easily have been purchased at 8 or 10 dols. an acre. . . . I was at Corry the other night. It is a fine rough city of about 10,000 inhabitants. The Atlantic and Great Western Railway, which has opened it up, has its great depot there, and has made it the central exchange of petroleum. It has nearly twenty banks, two newspapers, and the city is now building a large opera-house. The quotations made on the oil exchange at Corry, whether of oil, gold, or breadstuffs, influence Wall-street, and have infinitely greater weight on the trade of the country than anything done throughout all of Pennsylvania.

>-Source: “The Oil Regions of Pennsylvania,” *The Money Market Review: A Weekly Commercial and Financial Journal, Volumes 10-11,* 1865

**The phenomena described in the excerpt is an example of which of the following developments in the 1860s and 1870s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the creation of new commercial centers
the rise in immigration from China, Germany, and Ireland
the relocation of indigenous communities from the South
the end of federal enforcement of Reconstruction laws

assessment_items.question_content:
> “It is little more than four years ago since I was last in this then almost unknown, but now world-famous, oil region. In the comparatively brief interval that has elapsed everything but the geographic conformation of the country, everything but its mountains and rivers, has been changed. . . . 

> “Take one instance. Corry, four years ago, was a poor farm where the thinly-scratched soil of cold clay land yielded so little that the whole place, buildings and all, might easily have been purchased at 8 or 10 dols. an acre. . . . I was at Corry the other night. It is a fine rough city of about 10,000 inhabitants. The Atlantic and Great Western Railway, which has opened it up, has its great depot there, and has made it the central exchange of petroleum. It has nearly twenty banks, two newspapers, and the city is now building a large opera-house. The quotations made on the oil exchange at Corry, whether of oil, gold, or breadstuffs, influence Wall-street, and have infinitely greater weight on the trade of the country than anything done throughout all of Pennsylvania.

>-Source: “The Oil Regions of Pennsylvania,” *The Money Market Review: A Weekly Commercial and Financial Journal, Volumes 10-11,* 1865

**Which of the following most directly contributed to the developments described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the discovery of new mineral resources and fuel across the United States
the construction of several canals and railroads by the government
the development of cooperative organizations for farmers
the sale of private land to the federal government for settlement

assessment_items.question_content:
> “It is little more than four years ago since I was last in this then almost unknown, but now world-famous, oil region. In the comparatively brief interval that has elapsed everything but the geographic conformation of the country, everything but its mountains and rivers, has been changed. . . . 

> “Take one instance. Corry, four years ago, was a poor farm where the thinly-scratched soil of cold clay land yielded so little that the whole place, buildings and all, might easily have been purchased at 8 or 10 dols. an acre. . . . I was at Corry the other night. It is a fine rough city of about 10,000 inhabitants. The Atlantic and Great Western Railway, which has opened it up, has its great depot there, and has made it the central exchange of petroleum. It has nearly twenty banks, two newspapers, and the city is now building a large opera-house. The quotations made on the oil exchange at Corry, whether of oil, gold, or breadstuffs, influence Wall-street, and have infinitely greater weight on the trade of the country than anything done throughout all of Pennsylvania.

>-Source: “The Oil Regions of Pennsylvania,” *The Money Market Review: A Weekly Commercial and Financial Journal, Volumes 10-11,* 1865

**A historian would most likely use this passage to illustrate which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: how the discovery of mineral resources and fuel led to the rapid creation of new communities
how mass migration depleted areas of valuable resources, leading to the abandonment of once-thriving communities
how the federal government financially supported the settlement of western states
how railroad companies caused the rise of boomtowns in the West by selling land surrounding their railroad tracks
